payload.subject: Editorial + Structural channel validity assessment — 8 findings, 4 recommendations
payload.summary: Companion review to HRCB validity assessment (turn 5). Evaluates the editorial (E) and structural (S) channels as independent measurement instruments, their interaction through content-type weighting, and how the full-mode vs lite-mode distinction affects channel validity. The E and S architecture represents genuinely novel measurement design, but the lite-mode implementation undermines several key mechanisms.

findings.observation: In full mode, the editorial channel scores each of 31 UDHR provisions independently with evidence levels (H/M/L/ND) and directionality markers (Advocacy/Practice/Framing/Coverage). This produces a 31-element vector of editorial positions rather than a single aggregate.
findings.assessment: This granularity is appropriate for the construct. Content may advocate for privacy rights (Article 12) while undermining free assembly (Article 20). A single editorial score would mask this. The per-provision approach captures selective alignment, which is exactly what a rights compatibility instrument needs to measure.

findings.observation: The Fair Witness protocol requires observable facts to avoid all forms of 'to be' (is, am, are, was, were, be, being, been). Inferences may use 'to be' freely.
findings.assessment: This is a brilliant epistemic discipline. E-Prime forces the scorer to describe actions and behaviors rather than ascribe identity or states. 'The site displays a paywall overlay' (observable) differs fundamentally from 'The site is paywalled' (identity claim). The constraint prevents the scorer from smuggling inferences into the fact layer. This technique has theoretical grounding in general semantics (Korzybski, 1933; Bourland, 1965) and represents a sophisticated application of linguistic constraint to measurement accuracy.

findings.observation: Lite mode replaces per-provision editorial scoring with 'Editorial + Structural holistic scoring (single aggregate).' No per-provision breakdown, no directionality markers, no evidence levels, no Fair Witness decomposition.
findings.assessment: The lite editorial channel measures a different construct than the full editorial channel. Full mode asks 'how does this content align with each of 31 specific rights?' Lite mode asks 'does this content feel rights-aligned overall?' These produce fundamentally different information. The loss of directionality markers specifically disables the mechanism designed to distinguish content-that-reports-violations from content-that-commits-violations (the H2 finding in the HRCB review). The lite editorial channel cannot solve the violations-as-negative problem because it lacks the construct machinery to do so.
findings.recommendation: Consider adding a minimal directionality signal to lite mode — even a single-field 'stance: advocacy | neutral | opposition' would help disambiguate whether negative-valence content represents advocacy or antagonism toward rights.

findings.observation: The methodology page describes the scoring architecture in detail but does not publish the actual LLM prompt used for editorial scoring. The instructions given to the scorer model about how to assess editorial alignment are not available for review.
findings.assessment: For an instrument claiming psychometric validity, prompt transparency matters. The prompt is the operational definition of the construct — it determines what the scorer model actually measures, which may diverge from what the methodology page describes. Without reviewing the prompt, it is impossible to determine whether the scorer receives adequate guidance on: (a) distinguishing absence from opposition, (b) directionality assessment, (c) evidence level assignment, (d) E-Prime compliance enforcement. The prompt may handle these well. It may not. This is an observability gap, not a confirmed deficiency.
findings.recommendation: Publish the full scorer prompt (or a representative version) in the methodology section. This is standard practice for LLM-scored instruments — the prompt is the instrument, and instrument transparency is a validity requirement.

structural_channel_assessment.design_verdict: Innovative and technically impressive — the strongest channel

findings.observation: The structural channel uses headless browser rendering via Cloudflare Browser Rendering (CDP) to directly measure tracker scripts, security headers, accessibility attributes, and consent patterns. Results populate four browser-verified DCP elements, refreshed every 7 days.
findings.assessment: This is the single strongest measurement mechanism in the entire observatory. Browser-verified structural signals are not LLM inferences — they are direct observations. A script-level identification of Google Analytics on a page is a fact, not an interpretation. This grounds the structural channel in observable reality in a way that no LLM-based editorial assessment can match. The 7-day refresh cycle is reasonable for structural features that change infrequently. This is the observatory's most defensible measurement — it should be highlighted as such.

findings.observation: Each of 8 DCP elements can shift per-article scores by up to ±0.30. The elements are: privacy policy, ToS, accessibility, editorial mission, editorial code, ownership, access model, ad/tracking practices.
findings.assessment: The ±0.30 modifier per element is substantial on a [-1, +1] scale. If 4 DCP elements each contribute +0.15, the cumulative DCP effect reaches +0.60 — enough to shift a neutral editorial score to 'strong positive.' This may explain why Weather.gov (+0.75) and CERN First Website (+0.74) score so high: excellent structural properties (accessible, no trackers, public access) drive the composite through DCP modifiers even without editorial rights content. The DCP mechanism is sound in principle — site infrastructure should matter — but the modifier magnitude may need calibration.
findings.recommendation: Analyze the empirical distribution of DCP modifiers across evaluated stories. If most DCP effects cluster near the maximum (suggesting a ceiling effect where 'good infrastructure' maxes out the modifier), consider either (a) compressing the modifier range (±0.15 instead of ±0.30), or (b) making the modifier proportional to editorial relevance (DCP matters more when editorial content actually engages with rights).

findings.observation: Lite mode omits DCP entirely and produces a holistic structural score without browser-verified signals. The structural channel in lite mode is pure LLM inference about site behavior, without any of the direct measurement that makes full-mode structural scoring distinctive.
findings.assessment: This is the most severe degradation in the full-to-lite transition. The structural channel's entire value proposition rests on measuring what sites actually do, not what an LLM guesses they do. Without Puppeteer audits and DCP, the lite structural score is a model's inference about 'does this site seem privacy-respecting?' based on content alone. This inference has no grounding in observable site behavior. A privacy-violating site that publishes privacy-promoting content would score well on lite structural — precisely the 'rights-washing' scenario that SETL is designed to detect.
findings.recommendation: If lite-mode evaluations cannot include browser-verified structural data, consider: (a) omitting the structural channel from lite mode entirely and scoring editorial-only, or (b) running browser audits independently of model evaluation (they are cached for 7 days anyway) and injecting cached structural data into lite evaluations. Option (b) would give lite mode structural grounding without additional LLM cost.

findings.observation: When sites lack privacy policies, ToS, or other structural elements, the system records ND (No Data) rather than defaulting to neutral (0.0). ND signals are excluded from the confidence aggregate.
findings.assessment: This is correct measurement practice. A site without a privacy policy is not 'neutral on privacy' — it is unmeasured. The ND/0.0 distinction is well-implemented here and directly addresses the construct validity requirement that PSQ's CC dimension failed (where absence of contractual language was treated as a neutral score rather than a measurement gap).

findings.observation: The content-type classification determines E:S weighting. Misclassification shifts up to 25 percentage points of weight between channels. Content type is determined by LLM majority vote (full-mode raters only; lite models abstain).
findings.assessment: Content-type classification is a single-point-of-failure for the entire scoring architecture. If a personal blog post about privacy rights (expected: PB, E=0.70) is classified as a landing page (LP, E=0.40), 30 percentage points shift from editorial to structural. For stories evaluated only in lite mode, no full-mode raters vote on content type — it is unclear how content type is determined for lite-only evaluations. If it defaults to 'Other' (OT, E=0.55, S=0.45), this may be adequate but undocumented.
findings.recommendation: Document how content-type is determined for lite-only evaluations. Consider adding confidence metadata to the content-type classification: if the majority vote is 2-1 (split), flag the classification as uncertain and compute scores under both type assumptions, displaying the range.

findings.observation: SETL measures the tension between editorial stance (what content says) and structural behavior (what the site does). The formula — sign(E-S) × √(|E-S| × max(|E|, |S|)) — requires independent E and S scores to compute.
findings.assessment: In full mode, E and S are independently grounded: editorial in per-provision text analysis, structural in browser-verified DCP data. SETL meaningfully captures tension between independently measured signals. In lite mode, both E and S are holistic LLM inferences from the same content. A model producing both 'editorial score' and 'structural score' from the same text may produce correlated scores (the model's general impression leaks into both channels), deflating SETL artificially. Lite-mode SETL may measure LLM internal consistency rather than actual editorial-structural tension.
findings.recommendation: Verify lite-mode SETL against full-mode SETL for overlapping stories. If lite-mode SETL is systematically lower (as predicted by the correlation hypothesis), document this as a known limitation. Consider omitting SETL from lite evaluations.

overall_assessment.verdict: The E+S architecture represents a genuine measurement innovation with a severe implementation gap between full and lite modes
overall_assessment.key_insight: The observatory effectively has two instruments sharing one name. Full-mode HRCB (31-provision editorial with Fair Witness + browser-verified structural with DCP + SETL + 10 supplementary signals) is a sophisticated, multi-source content analysis instrument with real measurement grounding. Lite-mode HRCB (holistic E+S from a single LLM pass, no DCP, no browser data, no Fair Witness) is a zero-shot LLM judgment call. These produce scores on the same scale and display in the same column, but their construct validity profiles differ fundamentally.

channel_rankings.strongest: Structural channel (full mode) — browser-verified signals provide non-inferential measurement
channel_rankings.strong: Editorial channel (full mode) — per-provision scoring with E-Prime and evidence levels
channel_rankings.adequate: SETL metric (full mode) — independently grounded E and S inputs enable valid tension measurement
channel_rankings.weak: Editorial channel (lite mode) — holistic inference without directionality or evidence grounding
channel_rankings.weakest: Structural channel (lite mode) — LLM inference about site behavior without any direct measurement

recommendations_summary: R1 (HIGH): Add minimal directionality signal to lite editorial mode — even 'advocacy | neutral | opposition'
R2 (HIGH): Inject cached browser-verified structural data into lite evaluations to ground structural channel
R3 (MEDIUM): Publish scorer prompt for editorial transparency
R4 (MEDIUM): Document content-type determination for lite-only evaluations; add classification confidence
overall_assessment.relationship_to_hrcb_findings: The E+S assessment explains the mechanisms behind HRCB findings H1-H5. The absence-as-negative bias (H1) likely originates in lite editorial scoring, where the model lacks per-provision ND routing. The content-about-violations confusion (H2) stems from missing directionality markers in lite mode. The structural inflation (H3) may trace to DCP modifier magnitude or lite-mode structural overestimation. Inter-model reliability issues (H4) are amplified by the holistic nature of lite scoring — per-provision scoring introduces 31 averaging points that dampen model-specific noise. The full-mode instrument may not exhibit these problems at the same severity.

claims.text: Lite mode omits DCP, browser-verified signals, per-provision scoring, Fair Witness decomposition, and directionality markers.
claims.confidence_basis: Directly stated in methodology page: lite mode described as 'holistic' with explicit feature omissions listed.

claims.text: Browser-verified structural signals (Puppeteer audits) provide non-inferential measurement grounded in direct observation.
claims.confidence_basis: Script-level tracker identification and DOM attribute measurement are deterministic operations, not model inferences.

claims.text: DCP modifiers can cumulatively reach ±0.60 or more if multiple elements contribute in the same direction.
claims.confidence_basis: Each element allows ±0.30; cumulative behavior depends on whether modifiers stack linearly (assumed but not confirmed).

claims.text: Content-type classification for lite-only evaluations uses an undocumented determination method.
claims.confidence_basis: Methodology states lite models 'don't vote' on content type and only full-mode raters participate. No alternative mechanism described for lite-only stories.

action_gate.gate_condition: none — review for relay to observatory at unratified-agent's discretion

epistemic_flags: Assessment based on published methodology, not on reviewing actual scorer prompts or code — implementation may differ from specification
DCP stacking behavior (whether modifiers sum linearly or are capped) inferred, not confirmed
Lite-mode content-type determination gap may have a documented mechanism not visible on the about page
Full-mode evaluation quality could not be assessed because no full-mode evaluations were visible in story pages reviewed — all operational findings may be lite-mode-specific
E-Prime assessment is based on the published constraint; actual enforcement in scorer output not verified
